description:
You are an infrastructure engineer who has just finished writing a small Bash-based provisioning utility (not part of this exercise).  
Before committing your work, you want to:

1. Collect a short piece of Markdown documentation that other team members can quickly read.  
2. Run a **very light-weight “lint” check** that guarantees three required headers are present.  
3. Store the lint results in a log file for the CI system to archive later.

Your goal is therefore to create the documentation file, perform the lint check, and save the results.

Detailed requirements that the automated grader will look for:

A. Directory layout  
   • All artefacts must be placed beneath /home/user.  
   • Create a sub-directory named /home/user/docs (exact spelling, all lowercase).  

B. Markdown documentation  
   • Inside /home/user/docs create a file named provisioning.md (全部小写).  
   • The file must contain **exactly** the five lines listed below (newline after each line). Do not add extra spaces or blank lines.  
     1. “# Provisioning Utility”  
     2. “## Purpose”  
     3. “Automates baseline server setup.”  
     4. “## Usage”  
     5. “Run ./provision.sh with the desired flags.”  

C. Lint script  
   • Still inside /home/user/docs create an executable Bash script named md_lint.sh.  
   • The script must:  
     1. Read /home/user/docs/provisioning.md.  
     2. Verify that the file contains ALL THREE of the Markdown headers “# Provisioning Utility”, “## Purpose” and “## Usage”.  
     3. Output one line per check in the format:  

        OK – <HEADER>  

        …or…  

        MISSING – <HEADER>  

       (Case-sensitive, the actual angle brackets not included.)  
   • The script must write the same output to STDOUT **and** to /home/user/docs/md_lint.log (overwrite if it already exists).  
   • Make sure md_lint.sh has execute permission for the current user so that it can be run directly (e.g., ./md_lint.sh).  

D. What the agent should finally do  
   • Create the /home/user/docs directory.  
   • Populate provisioning.md with the five exact lines.  
   • Write md_lint.sh implementing the rules above and make it executable.  
   • Execute md_lint.sh so that /home/user/docs/md_lint.log is created.  

The test harness will check:

1. The presence of /home/user/docs/provisioning.md with exactly the required five lines in the correct order.  
2. The presence of /home/user/docs/md_lint.sh and that it is executable.  
3. The presence of /home/user/docs/md_lint.log that must contain exactly three lines, each either “OK – …” or “MISSING – …” for the three headers listed above.  
4. At least for the initial correct solution, all three lines are expected to start with “OK – ”.

You should only need around three or four shell commands in total to satisfy everything.
truth:
1. Expected file: /home/user/docs/provisioning.md  
   Content (including terminating newline at end of file):

# Provisioning Utility
## Purpose
Automates baseline server setup.
## Usage
Run ./provision.sh with the desired flags.

2. Expected file: /home/user/docs/md_lint.sh  
   Behaviour:  
   • Shell script using grep or similar.  
   • Must emit the following three lines when run on the correct provisioning.md:  
OK – # Provisioning Utility  
OK – ## Purpose  
OK – ## Usage  

   Script must redirect/tee this exact output to /home/user/docs/md_lint.log and STDOUT.

3. Expected file: /home/user/docs/md_lint.log with the three “OK” lines listed above, final newline after the third line.

4. /home/user/docs/md_lint.sh must have executable bit set (e.g., 755).

These are the precise artefacts the test suite will verify.
name: task_000000_f55d0d73
